title: Fraud / Abuse Detection — System Design
description: Design a risk engine that decides, in real time, whether an action (payment, login, signup) is fraudulent or abusive. The themes are fast inline decisioning (rules + ML), near-real-time velocity/graph features, a human-review loop, and a feedback loop that turns confirmed outcomes into better models.

nodes.label: Calling Service
nodes.description: Asks 'is this action risky?' inline (e.g. checkout, login, signup).

nodes.label: Risk Decision API
nodes.description: Synchronous decision: allow / block / challenge / review.

nodes.label: Rules Engine
nodes.description: Evaluates deterministic rules and velocity checks fast.

nodes.label: ML Scoring Model
nodes.description: Returns a risk score from features.

nodes.label: Event Stream
nodes.description: All actions/events; drives feature computation and offline analysis.

nodes.label: Entity / Link Graph
nodes.description: Links users/devices/cards/IPs to detect rings and shared identity.

nodes.label: Label / Outcome Store
nodes.description: Confirmed fraud/legit labels (incl. chargebacks) for training.

nodes.label: Offline Trainer
nodes.description: Retrains the model from labeled outcomes.

functional: Score an action's risk synchronously and return a decision (allow/block/challenge/review).
Combine deterministic rules and an ML risk model.
Use velocity and reputation features (this user/device/IP recently).
Detect coordinated abuse (fraud rings, sockpuppets) via entity links.
Route ambiguous cases to human review and learn from labeled outcomes.
nonFunctional: Low decision latency — it's inline on a user action (tens of ms).
High recall on fraud without too many false positives (block real users).
Adapt quickly: attackers change tactics, so rules/models must update fast.
Resilient: a scoring outage degrades to a safe default, not an outage of the host action.

capacity.label: Review fraction
capacity.note: Only ambiguous cases go to humans.

api.description: Score an action and return a decision synchronously.
Report an action/outcome event (feeds features and offline analysis).
Record a confirmed outcome (fraud/legit, chargeback) for training.

dataModel.name: decisions
dataModel.note: Audit record of each decision + the signals behind it.

patterns.name: Inline rules + ML decisioning
patterns.what: Combine fast deterministic rules with an ML score to a single decision synchronously.
patterns.whenToUse: Real-time risk decisions on a user action.

patterns.name: Streaming velocity features
patterns.what: Compute counters (events per entity per window) in near-real-time from the event stream.
patterns.whenToUse: Catching bursts/abuse that need fresh aggregates.

patterns.name: Entity link graph
patterns.what: Link users/devices/cards/IPs to surface rings and shared-identity abuse.
patterns.whenToUse: Coordinated fraud that single-entity signals miss.

patterns.name: Sync-vs-async decisioning
patterns.what: Block obvious fraud inline; flag ambiguous cases for async review or step-up challenge.
patterns.whenToUse: Balancing latency, recall, and false positives.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop labeling
patterns.what: Route ambiguous cases to analysts whose labels become training data.
patterns.whenToUse: When automated confidence is low and labels are scarce.

patterns.name: Feedback loop / retraining
patterns.what: Turn confirmed outcomes (chargebacks, reviews) into labels to retrain models.

steps.title: 1. Naive: A Few Static If-Rules Inline in the App (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. The calling service has a handful of hard-coded checks inline: is this card on a blocklist? Is the amount over $X? Is the IP in a banned country? If any fires, block; otherwise allow.

This is cheap, fast, and explainable — and it exposes the problems the rest of the design exists to solve. Static rules only catch fraud you already wrote a rule for: they miss fuzzy, novel, and coordinated fraud (a ring of fresh accounts each individually clean, a fraudster just under the amount threshold). They are crude — a single $X cutoff blocks legitimate big spenders (false positives) while letting smart fraud through. And because the rules are hard-coded in the app, adapting to a new attack means a code change and deploy, far too slow against an adversary that changes tactics daily.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: add an ML model that scores fuzzy risk from fresh behavioral features, link entities to catch rings, route ambiguous cases to humans, and feed confirmed outcomes back to keep learning.
steps.decisionPrompt: If your only checks are a blocklist and 'amount > $X', how do you catch a ring of fresh accounts that each look clean, and how fast can you react to a new attack?
whyNow: Establishing the static-rules baseline first makes it obvious why fuzzy ML scoring, fresh features, graph signals, and a feedback loop are needed — every later step removes a specific blind spot from here.

recap.after: A few hard-coded if-rules (blocklist, amount cap) decide allow/block inline in the app.
recap.newRisk: Static rules miss fuzzy and coordinated fraud, over-block legit users, and can't adapt without a redeploy.

traps.trap: Relying only on a few static if-rules baked into the application.
traps.why: They catch only known patterns, cause false positives with crude cutoffs, and can't adapt to a fast-moving adversary.
traps.instead: Combine rules with an ML score over fresh features, add graph signals, and close a feedback loop that retrains.

steps.title: 2. Inline Decisioning: Rules + ML
description: Risk is asked inline on a user action (checkout, login), so it must answer in tens of ms with a decision: allow, block, challenge (step-up auth), or send to review. The risk API combines two engines.

A rules engine handles fast, deterministic checks (blocklists, hard velocity limits, impossible-travel) — cheap, explainable, and instantly updatable. An ML model returns a risk score from features for the fuzzy cases rules can't capture.
Combine them: hard rules can short-circuit (block known-bad immediately); otherwise the score plus rule signals map to a decision with thresholds. Every decision records its reasons for auditability.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why combine a rules engine with an ML model instead of using only one?

concepts.definition: Deterministic rules for known patterns plus an ML score for fuzzy risk, combined into one decision.
concepts.whyItMatters: Rules give instant, explainable control; ML generalizes — together they cover more with fewer gaps.
concepts.example: Rule blocks a known-bad card instantly; model flags a subtle anomaly.

whyNow: The inline decision is the product; framing it as rules + ML with an explainable output is the core architecture.

recap.newRisk: The model is only as good as its features — and fraud needs fresh, behavioral features.

flows.name: Inline decision: rule short-circuit or ML score
flows.note: A hard rule (known-bad) blocks immediately; otherwise the model scores from fresh features and the score maps to allow/challenge/review.

steps.title: 3. Streaming Velocity Features
description: Most fraud signal is behavioral and recent: 'this card tried 20 merchants in 5 minutes', 'this IP created 100 accounts today'. These velocity/aggregate features must be fresh (seconds), so compute them in near-real-time.

Every action emits an event to a stream; a streaming aggregator maintains per-entity counters (per user/device/IP/card, per window) and reputation, writing them to a low-latency feature store the model/rules read at decision time.
Online/offline parity matters: the same feature definitions used at scoring must be used in training, or the model sees different inputs than it learned on.
steps.decisionPrompt: How does the model know this card was used at 20 merchants in the last 5 minutes, within a 20ms budget?

concepts.definition: Near-real-time per-entity counters/aggregates over recent windows.
concepts.whyItMatters: Catches bursty/coordinated abuse that static features miss; must be fresh and fast to read.
concepts.example: payments_last_5m for a card, served from the feature store in <5ms.

whyNow: Fresh behavioral features are what make fraud detection work; the stream->aggregate->feature-store path is the standard mechanism.

recap.after: Fresh velocity/reputation features feeding rules + model.
recap.newRisk: Sophisticated fraud is coordinated across many entities — single-entity features miss rings.

steps.title: 4. Entity Graph for Coordinated Abuse
description: Fraud rings share resources: many 'users' on one device, many cards behind one IP, recycled phone numbers. Single-entity features can't see this; an entity link graph connecting users/devices/cards/IPs/addresses can.

The aggregator maintains these links from events; graph features (cluster size, shared-device count, connection to known-bad entities) feed the model. A new account linked to a known fraud cluster is high-risk even with a clean individual history.
Graph signals are powerful for catching organized abuse and bot rings that look individually benign — a key differentiator from per-event scoring.
steps.decisionPrompt: A brand-new account looks clean individually. How might it still be obviously fraudulent?

concepts.definition: A graph linking shared identifiers (device, IP, card) to reveal clusters/rings.
concepts.whyItMatters: Surfaces coordinated abuse invisible to single-entity features.
concepts.example: New user shares a device with 50 chargeback accounts -> high risk.

whyNow: Graph features catch organized fraud that per-event scoring misses; it's the depth that separates a strong fraud answer.

recap.after: Ring/cluster detection via the entity graph.
recap.newRisk: Not every case is clear-cut — ambiguous ones need a path beyond instant allow/block.

steps.title: 5. Sync vs Async: Block, Challenge, Review
description: Not every decision should be a hard inline allow/block. Block only high-confidence fraud (false positives block real customers); for medium risk, issue a step-up challenge (2FA, ID check) inline; for ambiguous cases, allow-but-flag and send to async human review rather than guessing.
Analysts work a review queue with the decision's signals and the entity graph, then label the outcome. This human-in-the-loop both resolves hard cases and generates high-quality labels.
This sync/async split balances latency, recall, and false positives: instant for the obvious, challenge for the uncertain, async review for the genuinely ambiguous.
steps.decisionPrompt: A charge scores 0.6 — not clearly fraud, not clearly legit. What do you do with it?

concepts.definition: Inline block/challenge for confident cases; async review for ambiguous ones.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids blocking good users while still catching fraud and gathering labels.
concepts.example: Score>0.9 block; 0.5-0.9 challenge; flagged -> review queue.

whyNow: The decision-type split is the practical heart of a risk engine; conflating everything into block/allow causes false positives and missed fraud.

recap.newRisk: Attackers adapt; static rules/models decay — the system must keep learning.

traps.trap: Hard-blocking everything above a low threshold.
traps.why: Blocks many legitimate users (false positives), hurting the business more than the fraud.
traps.instead: Challenge medium risk and route ambiguous cases to review.

flows.name: Tiered outcome: block / challenge / review
flows.note: Confident fraud is blocked inline, medium risk gets a step-up challenge, and ambiguous cases are allowed-but-flagged to an analyst whose label feeds training.

steps.title: 5a. Decision Tiers and Thresholds
description: Not every score should be a hard allow/block — the tiers and their thresholds are a deliberate design. Block only high-confidence fraud (false positives turn away real customers and revenue). For the uncertain middle, issue a step-up challenge (2FA, ID check) inline so a legit user can prove themselves. For genuinely ambiguous cases, allow-but-flag to async review rather than guessing.

Thresholds are tuned against the business cost matrix: the cost of a false positive (blocked good user) vs a false negative (missed fraud) is rarely symmetric, and it differs by action (a $5 charge vs a $5,000 one). Calibrate the score and set per-action thresholds accordingly.
These cutoffs are not static — they're monitored and adjusted as fraud patterns and approval/chargeback rates shift, which is why the feedback loop (next step) matters.
steps.decisionPrompt: A charge scores 0.6. Block, challenge, or review? How do you set the cutoffs?

concepts.term: Tiered thresholds on a cost matrix
concepts.definition: Block / challenge / review bands set from the asymmetric cost of false positives vs false negatives, per action.
concepts.whyItMatters: It balances fraud caught against legit users blocked — the core business tradeoff.
concepts.example: score>0.9 block; 0.5-0.9 challenge; flagged -> review; thresholds vary by amount.

traps.trap: One global block threshold for all actions.
traps.why: It over-blocks low-risk actions and under-protects high-value ones; ignores the asymmetric cost.
traps.instead: Per-action, cost-calibrated tiers with a challenge band for the uncertain middle.

recap.after: Calibrated block/challenge/review tiers tuned to per-action costs.
recap.newRisk: Thresholds drift as fraud adapts — they need monitoring and the feedback loop.

steps.title: 6. The Feedback Loop: Labels and Retraining
description: Adversaries evolve, so the system must learn. Confirmed outcomes — chargebacks (fraud), analyst labels, user reports — flow into a label store. These labels train the next model version.

Label lag is the catch: a chargeback may arrive weeks after the charge, so training must align labels to the features as they were at decision time (point-in-time correctness), not current values. The decision audit record captures the features/score used, enabling this.

Offline training produces new model versions; deploy gradually (shadow/champion-challenger) and monitor for drift. Rules can be updated instantly for emerging patterns while the model retrains on the slower cadence.
steps.decisionPrompt: A chargeback confirms fraud 3 weeks after the charge. How do you use it to improve the model correctly?

concepts.definition: Joining late-arriving labels to the feature values as they were at decision time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids training leakage and reflects what the model actually saw.
concepts.example: Chargeback label joined to the action's snapshot features from 3 weeks ago.

whyNow: The feedback loop is what keeps the engine ahead of adversaries; point-in-time labeling is the subtle ML-systems correctness point.

recap.after: Outcomes -> point-in-time labels -> retrained models; rules updated instantly.
recap.newRisk: Inline scoring is on the critical path — it must be fast and fail safe at high volume.

steps.title: 7. Latency, Failure Modes, and Adversarial Robustness
description: Scoring is inline, so guard the latency budget: feature reads from an in-memory store, a bounded model, rules short-circuiting cheap cases, and caching repeated entity lookups. Heavy graph computation runs offline/near-real-time, not inline.

Fail safe: if the model or feature store is slow/down, fall back to rules-only (or a conservative default) rather than blocking the host action entirely — a risk-engine outage shouldn't take down checkout. Decisions are logged for audit and dispute resolution.

Adversarial robustness: don't leak why something was blocked to attackers (vague user messaging, detailed internal reasons), monitor for probing, and keep rules fast-updatable to respond to new attacks faster than retraining allows.
steps.decisionPrompt: The ML scoring service times out during checkout. Should checkout fail?
whyNow: Closing on latency, fail-safe behavior, and adversarial concerns makes the engine production-real — it must be fast, robust, and not become a SPOF.

recap.newRisk: Drift and adversary adaptation are continuous — bounded by monitoring, fast rules, and retraining.

bottlenecks.issue: Feature/model latency on the critical path.
bottlenecks.mitigation: In-memory features, bounded model, rule short-circuits, caching.
Fail open to rules-only / conservative default; never hard-fail the host action.

failureDrills.scenario: The ML model service is unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Decisioning falls back to the rules engine (and a safe default), keeping the host action working.

flows.name: Fail open to rules-only on scoring outage
flows.note: If the model or feature store times out, the risk API degrades to a rules-only conservative decision rather than failing the host action.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Real-Time Risk Engine
finalDesign.description: Callers ask the risk API inline; it combines a rules engine (fast, explainable, instantly updatable) with an ML model that scores using fresh features. Every action streams events to a near-real-time aggregator that maintains velocity/reputation features (online/offline parity) and an entity link graph for ring detection. Decisions are tiered — block confident fraud, challenge medium risk inline, allow-and-flag ambiguous cases to an analyst review queue whose labels (plus chargebacks) train the next model with point-in-time correctness. Scoring runs within a tight latency budget and fails open to rules-only on model/feature outage, with all decisions audited and attacker-facing reasons obscured.

nonFunctional.how: In-memory features, bounded model, rule short-circuits.
Decision records with reasons + model version.

say: Confirm inline real-time risk decisions with tiered outcomes.
Pin low latency, recall-vs-false-positives, and adaptability.
Streaming velocity features in a feature store.
Latency budget, fail-open fallback, adversarial concerns.
Tradeoffs: strictness vs conversion, sync vs async, label lag.
Mention drift monitoring and challenger models as extensions.

expectations: Scores with rules and/or a model inline.
May miss velocity features, graph, and feedback loop.
Reasons about point-in-time labeling and label lag.
Designs entity-graph features and fail-open fallback.
Discusses false-positive cost, adversarial adaptation, and drift precisely.

followUps: How do you avoid training leakage given that labels (chargebacks) arrive weeks later?
How do you keep false positives low without letting fraud through?
How would you detect and respond to a new attack faster than retraining allows?
How do you build entity-graph features without blowing the latency budget?
How do you measure the engine's effectiveness (and not just block rate)?
